questions.question: Which HTML element is used to embed external content such as a Flash animation or a PDF viewer plugin?
questions.explanation: The <embed> element embeds external content at a specified point in the document. It is commonly used for plugin-based content like Flash or PDF files.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the <param> element?
choices: It defines query parameters for a URL
It passes named parameters to an <object> element's plugin
It sets configuration values for the entire HTML page
It defines variables in embedded JavaScript
questions.explanation: The <param> element defines parameters for an <object> element's plugin. Each <param> has a name and value attribute that configure how the embedded content behaves.

questions.question: Which attribute on the <embed> element specifies the MIME type of the embedded content?
questions.explanation: The type attribute specifies the MIME type of the embedded resource (e.g., type="application/pdf"), which helps the browser determine how to handle the content.

questions.question: How does the <object> element differ from the <embed> element?
choices: The <object> element cannot embed multimedia content
The <object> element supports fallback content between its tags and <param> children
The <embed> element supports child elements but <object> does not
There is no difference; they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: The <object> element can contain fallback content (displayed if the object fails to load) and <param> elements for configuration. The <embed> element is a void element with no children.

questions.question: The ___ attribute on an <embed> or <object> element specifies the URL of the resource to embed.
questions.explanation: The src attribute provides the URL of the external resource. For <object>, the equivalent attribute is called data rather than src.

questions.question: On an <object> element, the URL of the embedded resource is specified with the ___ attribute instead of src.
questions.explanation: The <object> element uses the data attribute to specify the resource URL, unlike <embed> which uses src.
